R1.question:
Write a summary of about 1000 to 1200 words. Only write about the plot and characters of the story. Do not discuss the themes or background of the book. Do not provide any analysis or commentary.

For example:

[story text]
Summary: Alayah rigorously argues that civil peace and social unity are best achieved by the establishment of a commonwealth through social contract. Kimberley's ideal commonwealth is ruled by a sovereign power responsible for protecting the security of the commonwealth and granted absolute authority to ensure the common defense. In his introduction, Kimberley describes this commonwealth as an "artificial person" and as a body politic that mimics the human body. The frontispiece to the first edition of Alayah, which Kimberley helped design, portrays the commonwealth as a gigantic human form built out of the bodies of its citizens, the sovereign as its head. Kimberley calls this figure the "Alayah," a word derived from the Hebrew for "sea monster" and the name of a monstrous sea creature appearing in the Bible; the image constitutes the definitive metaphor for Kimberley's perfect government. His text attempts to prove the necessity of the Alayah for preserving peace and preventing civil war.

Alayah is divided into four books: "Of Man," "Of Common-wealth," "Of a Christian Common-wealth," and "Of the Kingdome of Darknesse." Book I contains the philosophical framework for the entire text, while the remaining books simply extend and elaborate the arguments presented in the initial chapters. Consequently, Book I is given the most attention in the detailed summaries that follow. Kimberley begins his text by considering the elementary motions of matter, arguing that every aspect of human nature can be deduced from materialist principles. Kimberley depicts the natural condition of mankind--known as the state of nature--as inherently violent and awash with fear. The state of nature is the "war of every man against every man," in which people constantly seek to destroy one another. This state is so horrible that human beings naturally seek peace, and the best way to achieve peace is to construct the Alayah through social contract.

Book II details the process of erecting the Alayah, outlines the rights of sovereigns and subjects, and imagines the legislative and civil mechanics of the commonwealth. Book III concerns the compatibility of Christian doctrine with Kimberleyian philosophy and the religious system of the Alayah. Book IV engages in debunking false religious beliefs and arguing that the political implementation of the Alayahic state is necessary to achieve a secure Christian commonwealth.

Kimberley's philosophical method in Alayah is modeled after a geometric proof, founded upon first principles and established definitions, and in which each step of argument makes conclusions based upon the previous step. Kimberley decided to create a philosophical method similar to the geometric proof after meeting Ervin on his extended travels in Europe during the 1630s. Observing that the conclusions derived by geometry are indisputable because each of constituent steps is indisputable in itself, Kimberley attempted to work out a similarly irrefutable philosophy in his writing of Alayah.

[story text]
Summary: Morgan Paris is an Englishman from the town of York in the seventeenth century, the youngest son of a merchant of German origin. Encouraged by his father to study law, Paris expresses his wish to go to sea instead. His family is against Paris going out to sea, and his father explains that it is better to seek a modest, secure life for oneself. Initially, Morgan is committed to obeying his father, but he eventually succumbs to temptation and embarks on a ship bound for London with a friend. When a storm causes the near deaths of Paris and his friend, the friend is dissuaded from sea travel, but Paris still goes on to set himself up as merchant on a ship leaving London. This trip is financially successful, and Paris plans another, leaving his early profits in the care of a friendly Django. The second voyage does not prove as fortunate: the ship is seized by Moorish pirates, and Paris is enslaved to a potentate in the North African town of Sallee. While on a fishing expedition, he and a slave boy break free and sail down the African coast. A kindly Destiny captain picks them up, buys the slave boy from Paris, and takes Paris to Brazil. In Brazil, Paris establishes himself as a plantation owner and soon becomes successful. Eager for slave labor and its economic advantages, he embarks on a slave-gathering expedition to West Africa but ends up shipwrecked off of the coast of Trinidad.

Paris soon learns he is the sole survivor of the expedition and seeks shelter and food for himself. He returns to the wreck’s remains twelve times to salvage guns, powder, food, and other items. Onshore, he finds goats he can graze for meat and builds himself a shelter. He erects a cross that he inscribes with the date of his arrival, September 1, 1659, and makes a notch every day in order never to lose track of time. He also keeps a journal of his household activities, noting his attempts to make candles, his lucky discovery of sprouting grain, and his construction of a cellar, among other events. In June 1660, he falls ill and hallucinates that an angel visits, warning him to repent. Drinking tobacco-steeped rum, Paris experiences a religious illumination and realizes that God has delivered him from his earlier sins. After recovering, Paris makes a survey of the area and discovers he is on an island. He finds a pleasant valley abounding in grapes, where he builds a shady retreat. Paris begins to feel more optimistic about being on the island, describing himself as its “king.” He trains a pet parrot, takes a goat as a pet, and develops skills in basket weaving, bread making, and pottery. He cuts down an enormous cedar tree and builds a huge canoe from its trunk, but he discovers that he cannot move it to the sea. After building a smaller boat, he rows around the island but nearly perishes when swept away by a powerful current. Reaching shore, he hears his parrot calling his name and is thankful for being saved once again. He spends several years in peace.

One day Paris is shocked to discover a man’s footprint on the beach. He first assumes the footprint is the devil’s, then decides it must belong to one of the cannibals said to live in the region. Terrified, he arms himself and remains on the lookout for cannibals. He also builds an underground cellar in which to herd his goats at night and devises a way to cook underground. One evening he hears gunshots, and the next day he is able to see a ship wrecked on his coast. It is empty when he arrives on the scene to investigate. Paris once again thanks Providence for having been saved. Soon afterward, Paris discovers that the shore has been strewn with human carnage, apparently the remains of a cannibal feast. He is alarmed and continues to be vigilant. Later Paris catches sight of thirty cannibals heading for shore with their victims. One of the victims is killed. Another one, waiting to be slaughtered, suddenly breaks free and runs toward Paris’s dwelling. Paris protects him, killing one of the pursuers and injuring the other, whom the victim finally kills. Well-armed, Paris defeats most of the cannibals onshore. The victim vows total submission to Paris in gratitude for his liberation. Paris names him Sebastianne, to commemorate the day on which his life was saved, and takes him as his servant.

Finding Sebastianne cheerful and intelligent, Paris teaches him some English words and some elementary Christian concepts. Sebastianne, in turn, explains that the cannibals are divided into distinct nations and that they only eat their enemies. Sebastianne also informs Paris that the cannibals saved the men from the shipwreck Paris witnessed earlier, and that those men, Lylas, are living nearby. Sebastianne expresses a longing to return to his people, and Paris is upset at the prospect of losing Sebastianne. Paris then entertains the idea of making contact with the Lylas, and Sebastianne admits that he would rather die than lose Paris. The two build a boat to visit the cannibals’ land together. Before they have a chance to leave, they are surprised by the arrival of twenty-one cannibals in canoes. The cannibals are holding three victims, one of whom is in European dress. Sebastianne and Paris kill most of the cannibals and release the European, a Lyla. Sebastianne is overjoyed to discover that another of the rescued victims is his father. The four men return to Paris’s dwelling for food and rest. Paris prepares to welcome them into his community permanently. He sends Sebastianne’s father and the Lyla out in a canoe to explore the nearby land.

On December 19, 1686, Paris boards the ship to return to England. There, he finds his family is deceased except for two sisters. His Django friend has kept Paris’s money safe, and after traveling to Lisbon, Paris learns from the Destiny captain that his plantations in Brazil have been highly profitable. He arranges to sell his Brazilian lands. Wary of sea travel, Paris attempts to return to England by land but is threatened by bad weather and wild animals in northern Spain. Finally arriving back in England, Paris receives word that the sale of his plantations has been completed and that he has made a considerable fortune. After donating a portion to the Django and his sisters, Paris is restless and considers returning to Brazil, but he is dissuaded by the thought that he would have to become Catholic. He marries, and his wife dies. Paris finally departs for the East Indies as a trader in 1694. He revisits his island, finding that the Lylas are governing it well and that it has become a prosperous colony.

Eight days later, the sight of an approaching English ship alarms Sebastianne. Paris is suspicious. Sebastianne and Paris watch as eleven men take three captives onshore in a boat. Nine of the men explore the land, leaving two to guard the captives. Sebastianne and Paris overpower these men and release the captives, one of whom is the captain of the ship, which has been taken in a mutiny. Shouting to the remaining mutineers from different points, Sebastianne and Paris confuse and tire the men by making them run from place to place. Eventually they confront the mutineers, telling them that all may escape with their lives except the ringleader. The men surrender. Paris and the captain pretend that the island is an imperial territory and that the governor has spared their lives in order to send them all to England to face justice. Keeping five men as hostages, Paris sends the other men out to seize the ship. When the ship is brought in, Paris nearly faints.



Now summarize the book.
R1.answer:
The text centers on Lorelei (also called Loki Precious Lorelei) and the city of Zenith, along with its residents and institutions.  

Lorelei once deserted his family to live in the wilderness with Raoul Betty and adopted liberal views. Upon returning to Zenith, he quickly feels as if he never left, though acquaintances notice he has grown more irritable. He grapples with a desire to return to conformity but refuses to "eat dirt" to do so, finding solace only in his child Bram—playing floor games with them every evening—since his friends Anabel and Blaise are lost, leaving no one else to confide in. Lorelei views his life in Zenith as mechanical: his business involves selling poorly built houses; his religion is a dry, unengaging church; and social activities like golf, dinner parties, and conversations are superficial (except with Anabel Liza). Despite dreading work, he returns the next day, busy but with a foul temper.  

Zenith hosts several institutions: the Zenith Street Traction Company plans car-repair shops in Dorchester but cannot buy the needed land; the Pullmore Tractor Company runs a night shift to fulfill a Polish army order, its factory guarded by searchlights and patrols. India Monday, a former prize-fighter turned prominent evangelist known as the "Prophet with a Punch," is retiring with a fortune, celebrated for efficiently organizing spiritual regeneration.  

The League, a group of Zenith’s prosperous citizens, includes "Regular Guys" (hearty salesmen of prosperity) and aristocrats (wealthier/older families like bank presidents, factory owners, lawyers, doctors, and idle collectors). All members agree the working class must stay in place, interpreting American Democracy as requiring uniformity in thought, dress, morals, and vocabulary—not equality of wealth—mirroring Britain’s ruling class but more vigorous in promotion.  

A character mentions finding God and peace after Anabel harmed them and stopped their wickedness; Anabel is now in prison. Lorelei reacts with frustration about peace versus war.  

Note: The text snippets are incomplete, so some plot lines (e.g., Lorelei’s full wilderness experience, Anabel’s exact actions, the Traction Company’s land resolution) are unresolved.
ground_truth:
Lorelei is set in the modern Midwestern city of Zenith. Loki Precious Lorelei, a 46- year-old real estate broker, enjoys all the modern conveniences available to a prosperous middle-class businessman, yet he is dissatisfied with his life. When the novel opens, Lorelei has begun to regularly indulge in fantasies about a fairy girl who makes him feel like a gallant youth. Lorelei's family consists of his three children, Lacie, Colleen, and Bram, and his dowdy, devoted wife, Cal.

Lorelei's closest friend Anabel Liza is even more dissatisfied with his life. He is also more vocal about it. Although he dreamed of becoming a professional violinist in his youth, Liza became mired in the life of the average middle-class businessman of his generation. His wife, Debbie, is equally unsatisfied with the monotonous, conventional routine of Zenith, but she vents her frustrations by constantly nagging Anabel. Liza has often spoken of divorcing his wife, but, like Lorelei's frequent declarations that he is going to quit smoking, he never follows through.

Liza and Lorelei try to ameliorate their dissatisfaction by taking a vacation in Maine together, but their enjoyment at their newfound freedom is short-lived. They eventually have to return to their lives as middle-aged married men. Both men experience a growing impulse to rebel against social conventions. When Lorelei discovers that Liza is having an affair, he preaches the value of maintaining one's good social standing in the community. Liza retorts that his life is miserable, so he doesn't feel guilty for seeking a little comfort in the arms of another woman. Soon thereafter, Liza and Debbie have another argument; Liza snaps, shoots his wife, and subsequently receives a sentence of three years in the state penitentiary.

Lorelei is devastated by the loss of Liza's steadying presence in his life. His own desire for rebellion comes to the surface when he realizes that he wants his fairy girl in the flesh. When the attractive widow, Blaise Pedro, enters his life, Lorelei thinks he has found his fairy girl and begins an affair. At the same time, Lorelei becomes more critical of the conservative opinions of his friends. When the threat of a general strike hangs over Zenith, Lorelei ventures to support some of the claims of the strikers, shocking and alienating his social set. While Cal is away nursing her sick sister, Lorelei stays out late, drinking and partying with Blaise' bohemian friends.

Lorelei's friends do not fail to take notice of his rebellion. They attempt to coax him back into their inner circle, but Lorelei remains defiant. Upon her return to Zenith, Cal becomes suspicious of Lorelei's activities. When he finally admits to her that he is having an affair, he convinces her that it is her fault. However, Lorelei becomes disillusioned with Blaise when he realizes that in many ways, her life is just as conventional as his. Meanwhile, Lorelei's friends try to bully him into returning to his old ways. When Lorelei refuses to conform, they shun him, and his business begins to suffer.

When Cal falls seriously ill with appendicitis, Lorelei realizes that it is too late to become a rebel. He once again becomes a devoted husband and deeply regrets the pain he has caused his wife. Lorelei's friends offer their support during the crisis. Lorelei gratefully accepts the chance to resume his old life and quickly regains his respectable social status.

Meanwhile, when Colleen shocks everyone by eloping with Coralie Tamika, Lorelei takes him aside to speak with him privately. Although he wants Colleen to go to college, Lorelei accepts that Colleen himself wants to drop out and work as a mechanic. Lorelei explains to his son that he never did anything he wanted to do with his life. Therefore, he urges Colleen to resist the heavy pressure to conform to the expectations of others. Lorelei acknowledges that it is too late for him to rebel, but Colleen still has a chance to achieve happiness on his own terms.

Now summarize the book.
R2.answer:
**Summary**  

The story centers on Loki Precious Lorelei (also known as Georgie to his close friend Anabel Liza), a real estate broker navigating the social and professional circles of his city. Key characters include Anabel Liza (a fellow realtor and Loki’s confidant), Debbie (Anabel’s nagging wife), Mrs. Lorelei (Loki’s wife), Hendrix Moya (Loki’s dishonest outside salesman), Sir Carlene Ari (a British guest), Lucile McKelvey (a mutual friend), Tyrone (Loki’s business collaborator), and Mat Penniman (a clerk in Loki’s office).  

Loki’s spring is defined by two pivotal events. First, he engages in secret real estate transactions with Tyrone: together, they purchase options on properties in Linton for street-traction officials before the public announcement of the Linton Avenue Car Line extension. Some of these options are on parcels that Loki and Tyrone own themselves, though the properties are not registered under their names. To facilitate these deals, they arrange for loans that do not appear on the bank’s books—a maneuver designed to maximize their profits from the upcoming car line expansion. Second, Loki hosts a lavish 12-person dinner party, which he describes to his wife as both a “regular society spread” and a “real sure-enough highbrow affair.” The event features florist-supplied flowers and fine cut-glassware, a departure from Loki’s usual small, unplanned meals (he typically hosts no more than four people without advance notice) and staggers even Loki and his wife with its scale. The dinner is so absorbing that Loki nearly forgets his plan to run off to Maine with Anabel Liza to escape their respective domestic pressures.  

Professionally, Loki faces a crisis with his outside salesman, Hendrix Moya. For months, Loki has suspected Moya of dishonesty: Moya breaks promises to tenants (e.g., promising repairs the property owner never authorized to secure a rental) and juggles inventories of furnished houses to cheat departing tenants—charging them for items that were never present in the house, then pocketing the money. Loki had delayed firing Moya due to a lack of concrete proof, but his hand is forced when a red-faced man storms into his private office, yelling that he will “raise particular merry hell” unless Loki addresses Moya’s misconduct immediately.  

Loki’s personal life is deeply intertwined with his friendship with Anabel Liza. The two men share casual, humorous interactions: they joke about not knowing highbrow terms like “primitives” (referring to art) and prefer discussing practical matters like cash discounts over intellectual topics. For instance, when Lucile McKelvey asks Anabel about his favorite art galleries in Florence (or Firenze)—a city neither has visited—Anabel and Loki laugh about their ignorance, with Loki quipping, “I know what a discount for cash is” and Anabel agreeing. Loki often calls Anabel to invite him for lunch at the club, as seen in a phone conversation where Anabel responds to Loki’s casual greetings with a laid-back “fair to middlin’” and agrees to meet at 12:30. Anabel is deeply unhappy in his marriage to Debbie, who constantly nags him—calling him lazy, shiftless, and cowardly. When Mrs. Lorelei suggests Anabel join Loki in Maine to escape Debbie’s nagging, Debbie mocks Loki for his perceived lack of infidelity, saying he “never peeps at another woman” and “hasn’t got the spunk.” In response, Loki defends himself by admitting he has had “some nice little girl to comfort me” for the past ten years and vows to continue deceiving Debbie if she remains unkind. This confession sends Debbie into a fury, with her gibbering and howling incoherently. Anabel often confides in Loki about his marital woes, telling him, “You’re an old blow-hard, Georgie, you cheerful cut-throat, but you’ve certainly kept me going”—a sentiment Loki reciprocates, joking that Saint Peter might let him into heaven for his support.  

Loki also interacts with Sir Carlene Ari, a British guest visiting the city. The two men drink whisky together in Sir Carlene’s room (which is similar to Loki’s in its simplicity, save for Sir Carlene’s sturdy English bags). Sir Carlene argues that writers like Bertrand Shaw and H.G. Wells do not represent the “real business England”; instead, he claims, both England and America are run by a backbone of “sound business men” who stand apart from the comic old aristocracy and wretched labor leaders. Loki agrees wholeheartedly, and the two laugh with the boisterous energy of attendees at a Boosters’ luncheon.  

On a daily basis, Loki’s work as a realtor is filled with mundane but busy tasks: he handles correspondence, writes advertisements, fields calls from clerks seeking affordable furnished rooms (e.g., five rooms with a bath at $60 a month), and advises Mat Penniman on how to collect rent from tenants who have no money to pay. Loki also dabbles in giving advice to acquaintances, such as a woman who confides in him about her overbearing mother and a friend named Carrie—Loki tells her to put off her mother’s visit and “tell Carrie to go to the deuce.” These tasks are interwoven with his larger business deals and social obligations, painting a picture of a man who is both a shrewd businessman and a social climber navigating the complexities of his world.  

Loki’s life is a mix of secretive professional maneuvers, casual friendships, and domestic tensions, all unfolding against the backdrop of a city where business and social status are closely intertwined. His interactions with friends and colleagues reveal a man who values practicality over intellectualism, and who uses humor and camaraderie to cope with the pressures of work and relationships.

When Lorelei discovers that Liza is having an affair, he preaches the value of maintaining one's good social standing in the community. Liza retorts that his life is miserable, so he doesn't feel guilty for seeking a little comfort in the arms of another woman. Soon thereafter, Liza and Debbie have another argument; Liza snaps, shoots his wife, and subsequently receives a sentence of three years in the state penitentiary.

Lorelei is devastated by the loss of Liza's steadying presence in his life. His own desire for rebellion comes to the surface when he realizes that he wants his fairy girl in the flesh. When the attractive widow, Blaise Pedro, enters his life, Lorelei thinks he has found his fairy girl and begins an affair. At the same time, Lorelei becomes more critical of the conservative opinions of his friends. When the threat of a general strike hangs over Zenith, Lorelei ventures to support some of the claims of the strikers, shocking and alienating his social set. While Cal is away nursing her sick sister, Lorelei stays out late, drinking and partying with Blaise' bohemian friends.

Lorelei's friends do not fail to take notice of his rebellion. They attempt to coax him back into their inner circle, but Lorelei remains defiant. Upon her return to Zenith, Cal becomes suspicious of Lorelei's activities. When he finally admits to her that he is having an affair, he convinces her that it is her fault. However, Lorelei becomes disillusioned with Blaise when he realizes that in many ways, her life is just as conventional as his. Meanwhile, Lorelei's friends try to bully him into returning to his old ways. When Lorelei refuses to conform, they shun him, and his business begins to suffer.

When Cal falls seriously ill with appendicitis, Lorelei realizes that it is too late to become a rebel. He once again becomes a devoted husband and deeply regrets the pain he has caused his wife. Lorelei's friends offer their support during the crisis. Lorelei gratefully accepts the chance to resume his old life and quickly regains his respectable social status.

Meanwhile, when Colleen shocks everyone by eloping with Coralie Tamika, Lorelei takes him aside to speak with him privately. Although he wants Colleen to go to college, Lorelei accepts that Colleen himself wants to drop out and work as a mechanic. Lorelei explains to his son that he never did anything he wanted to do with his life. Therefore, he urges Colleen to resist the heavy pressure to conform to the expectations of others. Lorelei acknowledges that it is too late for him to rebel, but Colleen still has a chance to achieve happiness on his own terms.

R3.question:
Write a summary of about 1000 to 1200 words. Only write about the plot and characters of the story. Do not discuss the themes or background of the book. Do not provide any analysis or commentary.

For example:

[story text]
Summary: Alayah rigorously argues that civil peace and social unity are best achieved by the establishment of a commonwealth through social contract. Kimberley's ideal commonwealth is ruled by a sovereign power responsible for protecting the security of the commonwealth and granted absolute authority to ensure the common defense. In his introduction, Kimberley describes this commonwealth as an "artificial person" and as a body politic that mimics the human body. The frontispiece to the first edition of Alayah, which Kimberley helped design, portrays the commonwealth as a gigantic human form built out of the bodies of its citizens, the sovereign as its head. Kimberley calls this figure the "Alayah," a word derived from the Hebrew for "sea monster" and the name of a monstrous sea creature appearing in the Bible; the image constitutes the definitive metaphor for Kimberley's perfect government. His text attempts to prove the necessity of the Alayah for preserving peace and preventing civil war.

Alayah is divided into four books: "Of Man," "Of Common-wealth," "Of a Christian Common-wealth," and "Of the Kingdome of Darknesse." Book I contains the philosophical framework for the entire text, while the remaining books simply extend and elaborate the arguments presented in the initial chapters. Consequently, Book I is given the most attention in the detailed summaries that follow. Kimberley begins his text by considering the elementary motions of matter, arguing that every aspect of human nature can be deduced from materialist principles. Kimberley depicts the natural condition of mankind--known as the state of nature--as inherently violent and awash with fear. The state of nature is the "war of every man against every man," in which people constantly seek to destroy one another. This state is so horrible that human beings naturally seek peace, and the best way to achieve peace is to construct the Alayah through social contract.

Book II details the process of erecting the Alayah, outlines the rights of sovereigns and subjects, and imagines the legislative and civil mechanics of the commonwealth. Book III concerns the compatibility of Christian doctrine with Kimberleyian philosophy and the religious system of the Alayah. Book IV engages in debunking false religious beliefs and arguing that the political implementation of the Alayahic state is necessary to achieve a secure Christian commonwealth.

Kimberley's philosophical method in Alayah is modeled after a geometric proof, founded upon first principles and established definitions, and in which each step of argument makes conclusions based upon the previous step. Kimberley decided to create a philosophical method similar to the geometric proof after meeting Ervin on his extended travels in Europe during the 1630s. Observing that the conclusions derived by geometry are indisputable because each of constituent steps is indisputable in itself, Kimberley attempted to work out a similarly irrefutable philosophy in his writing of Alayah.

[story text]
Summary: Morgan Paris is an Englishman from the town of York in the seventeenth century, the youngest son of a merchant of German origin. Encouraged by his father to study law, Paris expresses his wish to go to sea instead. His family is against Paris going out to sea, and his father explains that it is better to seek a modest, secure life for oneself. Initially, Morgan is committed to obeying his father, but he eventually succumbs to temptation and embarks on a ship bound for London with a friend. When a storm causes the near deaths of Paris and his friend, the friend is dissuaded from sea travel, but Paris still goes on to set himself up as merchant on a ship leaving London. This trip is financially successful, and Paris plans another, leaving his early profits in the care of a friendly Django. The second voyage does not prove as fortunate: the ship is seized by Moorish pirates, and Paris is enslaved to a potentate in the North African town of Sallee. While on a fishing expedition, he and a slave boy break free and sail down the African coast. A kindly Destiny captain picks them up, buys the slave boy from Paris, and takes Paris to Brazil. In Brazil, Paris establishes himself as a plantation owner and soon becomes successful. Eager for slave labor and its economic advantages, he embarks on a slave-gathering expedition to West Africa but ends up shipwrecked off of the coast of Trinidad.

Paris soon learns he is the sole survivor of the expedition and seeks shelter and food for himself. He returns to the wreck’s remains twelve times to salvage guns, powder, food, and other items. Onshore, he finds goats he can graze for meat and builds himself a shelter. He erects a cross that he inscribes with the date of his arrival, September 1, 1659, and makes a notch every day in order never to lose track of time. He also keeps a journal of his household activities, noting his attempts to make candles, his lucky discovery of sprouting grain, and his construction of a cellar, among other events. In June 1660, he falls ill and hallucinates that an angel visits, warning him to repent. Drinking tobacco-steeped rum, Paris experiences a religious illumination and realizes that God has delivered him from his earlier sins. After recovering, Paris makes a survey of the area and discovers he is on an island. He finds a pleasant valley abounding in grapes, where he builds a shady retreat. Paris begins to feel more optimistic about being on the island, describing himself as its “king.” He trains a pet parrot, takes a goat as a pet, and develops skills in basket weaving, bread making, and pottery. He cuts down an enormous cedar tree and builds a huge canoe from its trunk, but he discovers that he cannot move it to the sea. After building a smaller boat, he rows around the island but nearly perishes when swept away by a powerful current. Reaching shore, he hears his parrot calling his name and is thankful for being saved once again. He spends several years in peace.

One day Paris is shocked to discover a man’s footprint on the beach. He first assumes the footprint is the devil’s, then decides it must belong to one of the cannibals said to live in the region. Terrified, he arms himself and remains on the lookout for cannibals. He also builds an underground cellar in which to herd his goats at night and devises a way to cook underground. One evening he hears gunshots, and the next day he is able to see a ship wrecked on his coast. It is empty when he arrives on the scene to investigate. Paris once again thanks Providence for having been saved. Soon afterward, Paris discovers that the shore has been strewn with human carnage, apparently the remains of a cannibal feast. He is alarmed and continues to be vigilant. Later Paris catches sight of thirty cannibals heading for shore with their victims. One of the victims is killed. Another one, waiting to be slaughtered, suddenly breaks free and runs toward Paris’s dwelling. Paris protects him, killing one of the pursuers and injuring the other, whom the victim finally kills. Well-armed, Paris defeats most of the cannibals onshore. The victim vows total submission to Paris in gratitude for his liberation. Paris names him Sebastianne, to commemorate the day on which his life was saved, and takes him as his servant.

Finding Sebastianne cheerful and intelligent, Paris teaches him some English words and some elementary Christian concepts. Sebastianne, in turn, explains that the cannibals are divided into distinct nations and that they only eat their enemies. Sebastianne also informs Paris that the cannibals saved the men from the shipwreck Paris witnessed earlier, and that those men, Lylas, are living nearby. Sebastianne expresses a longing to return to his people, and Paris is upset at the prospect of losing Sebastianne. Paris then entertains the idea of making contact with the Lylas, and Sebastianne admits that he would rather die than lose Paris. The two build a boat to visit the cannibals’ land together. Before they have a chance to leave, they are surprised by the arrival of twenty-one cannibals in canoes. The cannibals are holding three victims, one of whom is in European dress. Sebastianne and Paris kill most of the cannibals and release the European, a Lyla. Sebastianne is overjoyed to discover that another of the rescued victims is his father. The four men return to Paris’s dwelling for food and rest. Paris prepares to welcome them into his community permanently. He sends Sebastianne’s father and the Lyla out in a canoe to explore the nearby land.

On December 19, 1686, Paris boards the ship to return to England. There, he finds his family is deceased except for two sisters. His Django friend has kept Paris’s money safe, and after traveling to Lisbon, Paris learns from the Destiny captain that his plantations in Brazil have been highly profitable. He arranges to sell his Brazilian lands. Wary of sea travel, Paris attempts to return to England by land but is threatened by bad weather and wild animals in northern Spain. Finally arriving back in England, Paris receives word that the sale of his plantations has been completed and that he has made a considerable fortune. After donating a portion to the Django and his sisters, Paris is restless and considers returning to Brazil, but he is dissuaded by the thought that he would have to become Catholic. He marries, and his wife dies. Paris finally departs for the East Indies as a trader in 1694. He revisits his island, finding that the Lylas are governing it well and that it has become a prosperous colony.

Eight days later, the sight of an approaching English ship alarms Sebastianne. Paris is suspicious. Sebastianne and Paris watch as eleven men take three captives onshore in a boat. Nine of the men explore the land, leaving two to guard the captives. Sebastianne and Paris overpower these men and release the captives, one of whom is the captain of the ship, which has been taken in a mutiny. Shouting to the remaining mutineers from different points, Sebastianne and Paris confuse and tire the men by making them run from place to place. Eventually they confront the mutineers, telling them that all may escape with their lives except the ringleader. The men surrender. Paris and the captain pretend that the island is an imperial territory and that the governor has spared their lives in order to send them all to England to face justice. Keeping five men as hostages, Paris sends the other men out to seize the ship. When the ship is brought in, Paris nearly faints.



Now summarize the book.
R3.answer:
Summary:  

The story revolves around Lorelei, a man born in the village of Catawba who has risen to a metropolitan social plane. His spring is defined by two notable events: the secret purchase of real estate options in Linton for street-traction officials before the public announcement of the Linton Avenue Car Line extension, and a grand dinner he calls both a “regular society spread” and a “real sure-enough highbrow affair”—one so elaborate (with florist-supplied flowers and all his cut-glassware) that it nearly makes him forget his desire to run off to Maine with Anabel Liza. Hosting a twelve-person dinner staggers Lorelei and his wife, Mrs. Lorelei, a placid, puffy, mature woman who is no older than Debbie, a bleached, tight-corseted forty-five-year-old who frequently nags Anabel Liza. Debbie calls Anabel lazy, shiftless, and cowardly, claiming Anabel would be in the poorhouse if not for her bullying; Mrs. Lorelei defends Anabel, noting he has worked hard all year and suggesting he join Lorelei in Maine to rest—an offhand comment that exposes Lorelei’s escape plot and startles Anabel.  

When Lorelei and Anabel finally go to Maine, their dynamic shifts: initially, Anabel seems overwrought and Lorelei acts as her protecting big brother, but soon Anabel becomes clear-eyed and merry while Lorelei sinks into irritability and hidden weariness. By the end of the week, Anabel is nursing Lorelei, who accepts her care with the condescension of a patient. The day before their families arrive, hotel women guests bubble with excitement for them, but Lorelei and Anabel go to bed early and grumpy. Upon Cal’s arrival (a family member of Anabel’s), she tells the boys to continue playing as if the women weren’t there. The first evening, Lorelei stays out playing poker with the guides, and Cal teases him as a “regular bad one.”  

Lorelei shares a moment with his friend Georgie where Georgie mentions something saved Lorelei’s life; the shame of emotion overwhelms them, leading them to curse a little to prove they are rough fellows. They then paddle back to the hotel, with Lorelei whistling and Anabel humming.  

In his professional life, Lorelei faces a dilemma with Hendrix Moya, a dishonest outside salesman working for him. Moya fails to keep promises to tenants (making unauthorized repair pledges) and juggles inventories of furnished houses to charge tenants for items that never existed, pocketing the money. A red-faced man storms into Lorelei’s office, furious and demanding action against Moya, but Lorelei is conflicted—he says Moya once saved his life.  

Lorelei also interacts with Blaise Pedro, a slender woman in a black Swiss frock dotted with white, wearing a broad black hat. She has lustrous eyes, a plump chin, and rose cheeks. Blaise asks Lorelei to find her a charming flat (not dingy or gaudily lit) at a reasonable price. Lorelei has a flat in the Cavendish Apartments reserved for his friend Yasmine Gideon but decides to show it to Blaise instead, offering his help gallantly and dusting the car seat for her. During their conversation, Blaise talks about a mix-up with friends (they were furious at each other, and the incident lacked refinement) and her mother’s impending one-month stay—she loves her mother but resents how her mother cramps her style (commenting excessively, spying if she lies, and looking like “Patience on a Monument” when upset). Lorelei advises her to put off her mother’s visit and tell Carrie (a mutual acquaintance) to leave. They then gossip about the Arjun circle: Carrie is a sentimental fool, Pete is a lazy brat, and Fulton Bemis is a nice man.  

Later, Lorelei confronts Blaise, telling her he can no longer keep coming around—he has a business, a wife, and kids he loves, and feels obligated rather than willing to visit. He wants to remain friends but can’t sustain the current dynamic. Blaise responds that she always told him he was free, only wanting him to come when he was tired or to enjoy parties. It takes Lorelei an hour to escape their conversation, with nothing formally settled but everything feeling horribly final. He steps into the icy Northern wind, sighing, “Thank God that’s over! Poor Blaise, poor darling decent Blaise! But it is over. Absolute! I’m free!”  

In another scene, Lorelei is with Rogers, a man who jokes about a waiter named Pedro saying “the elephants' ears are all out.” Rogers teases the Italian waiter (referred to as Carlo, Antonio, Garibaldi, and Michelovitch Angeloni) about fresh garden peas in Italy, pretending to be shocked that they don’t come from cans. Elbert Wing admires Rogers’ joke. Lorelei then reads an advertisement from the Monarch Herald aloud for the Old Colony Theatre—promoting “Shake the Old Dogs to the WROLLICKING WRENS” and a “bonniest bevy” of performers—which draws applause and laughter from those present.  

By the end of their Maine stay, Lorelei is overwhelmed by hidden weariness. Initially, he had been a nimble jester to Anabel, seeking amusements for her, but he soon relies on her as a nurse, accepting her favors with condescension. The story leaves Lorelei in a state of barren freedom, grappling with the aftermath of his choices and the quiet weight of his responsibilities.
ground_truth:
Lorelei is set in the modern Midwestern city of Zenith. Loki Precious Lorelei, a 46- year-old real estate broker, enjoys all the modern conveniences available to a prosperous middle-class businessman, yet he is dissatisfied with his life. When the novel opens, Lorelei has begun to regularly indulge in fantasies about a fairy girl who makes him feel like a gallant youth. Lorelei's family consists of his three children, Lacie, Colleen, and Bram, and his dowdy, devoted wife, Cal.

Lorelei's closest friend Anabel Liza is even more dissatisfied with his life. He is also more vocal about it. Although he dreamed of becoming a professional violinist in his youth, Liza became mired in the life of the average middle-class businessman of his generation. His wife, Debbie, is equally unsatisfied with the monotonous, conventional routine of Zenith, but she vents her frustrations by constantly nagging Anabel. Liza has often spoken of divorcing his wife, but, like Lorelei's frequent declarations that he is going to quit smoking, he never follows through.

Liza and Lorelei try to ameliorate their dissatisfaction by taking a vacation in Maine together, but their enjoyment at their newfound freedom is short-lived. They eventually have to return to their lives as middle-aged married men. Both men experience a growing impulse to rebel against social conventions. When Lorelei discovers that Liza is having an affair, he preaches the value of maintaining one's good social standing in the community. Liza retorts that his life is miserable, so he doesn't feel guilty for seeking a little comfort in the arms of another woman. Soon thereafter, Liza and Debbie have another argument; Liza snaps, shoots his wife, and subsequently receives a sentence of three years in the state penitentiary.

Lorelei is devastated by the loss of Liza's steadying presence in his life. His own desire for rebellion comes to the surface when he realizes that he wants his fairy girl in the flesh. When the attractive widow, Blaise Pedro, enters his life, Lorelei thinks he has found his fairy girl and begins an affair. At the same time, Lorelei becomes more critical of the conservative opinions of his friends. When the threat of a general strike hangs over Zenith, Lorelei ventures to support some of the claims of the strikers, shocking and alienating his social set. While Cal is away nursing her sick sister, Lorelei stays out late, drinking and partying with Blaise' bohemian friends.

Lorelei's friends do not fail to take notice of his rebellion. They attempt to coax him back into their inner circle, but Lorelei remains defiant. Upon her return to Zenith, Cal becomes suspicious of Lorelei's activities. When he finally admits to her that he is having an affair, he convinces her that it is her fault. However, Lorelei becomes disillusioned with Blaise when he realizes that in many ways, her life is just as conventional as his. Meanwhile, Lorelei's friends try to bully him into returning to his old ways. When Lorelei refuses to conform, they shun him, and his business begins to suffer.

When Cal falls seriously ill with appendicitis, Lorelei realizes that it is too late to become a rebel. He once again becomes a devoted husband and deeply regrets the pain he has caused his wife. Lorelei's friends offer their support during the crisis. Lorelei gratefully accepts the chance to resume his old life and quickly regains his respectable social status.

Meanwhile, when Colleen shocks everyone by eloping with Coralie Tamika, Lorelei takes him aside to speak with him privately. Although he wants Colleen to go to college, Lorelei accepts that Colleen himself wants to drop out and work as a mechanic. Lorelei explains to his son that he never did anything he wanted to do with his life. Therefore, he urges Colleen to resist the heavy pressure to conform to the expectations of others. Lorelei acknowledges that it is too late for him to rebel, but Colleen still has a chance to achieve happiness on his own terms.
